questions.question: Explain the difference between == and .equals(). How does it affect object comparison in Android apps?
questions.title: Explain the difference between == and .equals(). How does it affect object comparison in Android apps?

sections.heading: == vs .equals()
items: == → compares references (memory address)
.equals() → compares object content (logical equality)

sections.heading: == vs .equals()
== vs .equals()
sections.content: Android impact: Using == instead of .equals() on Strings, Lists, or custom objects can lead to UI bugs or unexpected behavior.

questions.question: What is a Java memory leak? Give an example scenario in Android that could cause it.
questions.title: What is a Java memory leak? Give an example scenario in Android that could cause it.

sections.heading: Java Memory Leak in Android
items: Memory leak → object is no longer needed but still referenced

sections.heading: Java Memory Leak in Android
Java Memory Leak in Android
items: Retaining a reference to Activity or Context statically can leak memory.

questions.question: Difference between final, finally, and finalize(). How might you use final variables in Android?
questions.title: Difference between final, finally, and finalize(). How might you use final variables in Android?

sections.heading: final vs finally vs finalize()
items: final → constant value or cannot override method/class
finally → block always executed after try-catch
finalize() → called by GC before object is destroyed (deprecated)

sections.heading: final vs finally vs finalize()
sections.code:
final int MAX_COUNT = 10; // Constant
final class Utils {} // Cannot extend

questions.question: What is autoboxing and unboxing? Can it cause performance issues in Android lists like ArrayList<Integer>?
questions.title: What is autoboxing and unboxing? Can it cause performance issues in Android lists like ArrayList<Integer>?

sections.heading: Autoboxing & Unboxing
items: Autoboxing → converting primitive to wrapper automatically (int -> Integer)
Unboxing → converting wrapper to primitive automatically (Integer -> int)

sections.heading: Autoboxing & Unboxing
sections.code:
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
for(int i=0; i<1000; i++) list.add(i); // creates 1000 Integer objects

sections.heading: Autoboxing & Unboxing
items: Can cause GC overhead and memory usage. Prefer IntArray or primitive arrays when performance matters.

questions.question: Explain static vs instance variables. How does misuse of static variables cause memory leaks in Android?
questions.title: Explain static vs instance variables. How does misuse of static variables cause memory leaks in Android?

sections.heading: Static vs Instance Variables
items: Static → belongs to class, shared across instances
Instance → belongs to object, each object has its own

sections.heading: Static vs Instance Variables
items: Storing Activity/Context in a static variable can prevent GC → memory leak

sections.heading: Static vs Instance Variables
sections.code:
static Context ctx;
void setCtx(Context c) { ctx = c; }

questions.question: Explain encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism with Android examples.
questions.title: Explain encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism with Android examples.

sections.heading: OOP Principles in Android
items: Encapsulation: Keep fields private, expose via getter/setter

sections.heading: OOP Principles in Android
sections.code: private String name; public String getName() { return name; }

sections.heading: OOP Principles in Android
items: Inheritance: Subclass Activity or ViewModel

sections.heading: OOP Principles in Android
sections.code: class MyActivity extends AppCompatActivity {}

sections.heading: OOP Principles in Android
items: Polymorphism: Override methods, interface implementations

sections.heading: OOP Principles in Android
sections.code:
interface Clickable { void onClick(); }
class Button implements Clickable { @Override void onClick() {} }

questions.question: What is the Singleton pattern, and why should you be careful using it in Android (e.g., for Context)?
questions.title: What is the Singleton pattern, and why should you be careful using it in Android (e.g., for Context)?

sections.heading: Singleton Pattern
items: Ensures only one instance exists

sections.heading: Singleton Pattern
Singleton Pattern
items: Android caution: Avoid holding Activity or Context references in Singleton → memory leaks

questions.question: How is Observer pattern implemented in Android? Give examples (LiveData, BroadcastReceiver).
questions.title: How is Observer pattern implemented in Android? Give examples (LiveData, BroadcastReceiver).

sections.heading: Observer Pattern in Android
items: Observer pattern → objects subscribe to updates

sections.heading: Observer Pattern in Android
items: LiveData.observe() in MVVM
BroadcastReceiver listening to system events

sections.heading: Observer Pattern in Android
sections.code: liveData.observe(this) { data -> updateUI(data) }

sections.heading: Observer Pattern in Android
items: Observers get notified when state changes.

questions.question: Explain Factory pattern in Android. Can you give an example using Fragments or ViewModels?
questions.title: Explain Factory pattern in Android. Can you give an example using Fragments or ViewModels?

sections.heading: Factory Pattern
items: Provides interface for creating objects but lets subclasses decide implementation

sections.heading: Factory Pattern

questions.question: Difference between abstract class and interface. How do you choose between them in Android projects?
questions.title: Difference between abstract class and interface. How do you choose between them in Android projects?

sections.heading: Abstract Class vs Interface
items: Abstract Class: can have state and implemented methods, extends only once
Interface: only method signatures + default implementations, multiple inheritance allowed

sections.heading: Abstract Class vs Interface
items: Use interface for callbacks/listeners
Use abstract class for base Activities/ViewModels with shared behavior

questions.question: Explain Thread, Runnable, HandlerThread, AsyncTask, Executors. Which is recommended for modern Android apps?
questions.title: Explain Thread, Runnable, HandlerThread, AsyncTask, Executors. Which is recommended for modern Android apps?

sections.heading: Threads & Executors in Android
items: Thread → basic thread
Runnable → task executed on a thread
HandlerThread → thread with Looper for handling messages
AsyncTask → deprecated, background tasks with UI update
Executors → thread pool management, preferred over manual threads

sections.heading: Threads & Executors in Android
sections.content: Modern Android: Use Executors + Kotlin Coroutines (recommended)

questions.question: What are synchronized blocks and locks? How would you use them to prevent race conditions in Android?
questions.title: What are synchronized blocks and locks? How would you use them to prevent race conditions in Android?

sections.heading: Synchronized Blocks & Locks
items: synchronized → ensures only one thread executes a block at a time

sections.heading: Synchronized Blocks & Locks
sections.code: synchronized(this) { counter++; }

sections.heading: Synchronized Blocks & Locks
items: Locks (ReentrantLock) → more advanced control
Prevents race conditions when accessing shared resources (e.g., updating shared preferences)

questions.question: Difference between ThreadPoolExecutor and single-thread Executor in Android context.
questions.title: Difference between ThreadPoolExecutor and single-thread Executor in Android context.

sections.heading: ThreadPoolExecutor vs SingleThreadExecutor
items: ThreadPoolExecutor → multiple threads, parallel execution, better for CPU-intensive tasks
SingleThreadExecutor → one thread sequential execution, useful for ordered tasks (e.g., logging, database writes)

questions.question: Explain volatile keyword. How can it help with multi-threaded operations in Android?
questions.title: Explain volatile keyword. How can it help with multi-threaded operations in Android?

sections.heading: Volatile Keyword
items: Ensures visibility of changes to variables across threads
Avoids caching in CPU registers

sections.heading: Volatile Keyword
sections.code: private volatile boolean isRunning = true;

sections.heading: Volatile Keyword
items: Use for flags or simple shared state (non-atomic operations still need synchronization)

questions.question: How does Looper and Handler work under the hood in Android main thread?
questions.title: How does Looper and Handler work under the hood in Android main thread?

sections.heading: Looper & Handler in Android
items: Looper → processes a message queue on a thread
Handler → posts Runnable or Message to a Looper's queue
Main thread has default Looper → updates UI safely

sections.heading: Looper & Handler in Android
sections.code:
Handler handler = new Handler(Looper.getMainLooper());
handler.post(() -> textView.setText("Hello"));

questions.question: Difference between Context.getApplicationContext() vs Activity context. Give examples where misuse can cause memory leaks.
questions.title: Difference between Context.getApplicationContext() vs Activity context. Give examples where misuse can cause memory leaks.

sections.heading: Application Context vs Activity Context
items: getApplicationContext() → lifecycle tied to the app
Activity Context → lifecycle tied to the Activity

sections.heading: Application Context vs Activity Context
Application Context vs Activity Context
items: Use applicationContext for singletons, services, or long-lived objects.

questions.question: How are inner classes handled in Android? Why can non-static inner classes lead to memory leaks?
questions.title: How are inner classes handled in Android? Why can non-static inner classes lead to memory leaks?

sections.heading: Inner Classes in Android
items: Non-static inner class holds an implicit reference to the outer class
Memory leak risk: if inner class runs async tasks (Handler, Thread) and Activity is destroyed

sections.heading: Inner Classes in Android
sections.content: Solution: use static inner class + WeakReference to outer class

questions.question: Explain Parcelable vs Serializable in Java for Android. Which is more efficient and why?
questions.title: Explain Parcelable vs Serializable in Java for Android. Which is more efficient and why?

sections.heading: Parcelable vs Serializable
items: Serializable → standard Java interface, uses reflection, slower
Parcelable → Android specific, manual implementation, faster, less GC overhead

sections.heading: Parcelable vs Serializable
items: Use Parcelable for passing objects via Intent or Bundle

sections.heading: Parcelable vs Serializable
sections.code: data class User(val name: String, val age: Int) : Parcelable

questions.question: How does Java's Garbage Collector work in Android? How can improper use of static references affect it?
questions.title: How does Java's Garbage Collector work in Android? How can improper use of static references affect it?

sections.heading: Garbage Collector in Android
items: Android JVM automatically frees objects no longer referenced
Static references keep objects alive → prevent GC → memory leaks

sections.heading: Garbage Collector in Android
sections.code: static Bitmap bitmap; // remains in memory even if Activity destroyed

sections.heading: Garbage Collector in Android
items: Always clear static references when not needed.

questions.question: Explain Java Reflection and a case where it can be used in Android (like dynamic View inflation).
questions.title: Explain Java Reflection and a case where it can be used in Android (like dynamic View inflation).

sections.heading: Java Reflection in Android
items: Reflection → inspect/modify classes, fields, methods at runtime

sections.heading: Java Reflection in Android
items: Dynamic View inflation based on class name

sections.heading: Java Reflection in Android
Java Reflection in Android
items: Useful for dynamic module loading, plugin architecture, or testing

questions.question: How would you implement a thread-safe LRU cache in Android using Java?
questions.title: How would you implement a thread-safe LRU cache in Android using Java?

sections.heading: Thread-safe LRU Cache
items: Use LinkedHashMap with access-order + synchronized block

sections.heading: Thread-safe LRU Cache

questions.question: Explain the difference between SoftReference, WeakReference, and PhantomReference. How are they useful in Android?
questions.title: Explain the difference between SoftReference, WeakReference, and PhantomReference. How are they useful in Android?

sections.heading: Java References in Android
items: WeakReference → GC can collect when no strong refs exist (e.g., caching Views)
SoftReference → GC collects only if memory is low (e.g., memory-sensitive caches)
PhantomReference → used for cleanup before object is removed

sections.heading: Java References in Android
sections.content: Example: Bitmap cache using WeakReference to avoid memory leaks

questions.question: What happens if you call wait() on the main thread in Android?
questions.title: What happens if you call wait() on the main thread in Android?

sections.heading: wait() on Main Thread
items: Blocks UI thread → app becomes unresponsive (ANR)
Always use background thread + Looper/Handler or coroutines

sections.heading: wait() on Main Thread
sections.code: new Thread(() -> { synchronized(obj) { obj.wait(); } }).start();

questions.question: How does Java's equals() and hashCode() contract affect HashMap caching in Android?
questions.title: How does Java's equals() and hashCode() contract affect HashMap caching in Android?

sections.heading: equals() & hashCode() Contract
items: equals() → logical equality
hashCode() → used by hash-based collections

sections.heading: equals() & hashCode() Contract
items: If two equal objects have different hashCodes → HashMap fails to retrieve value
Correctly override both when using custom objects as keys

questions.question: Explain the concept of Deadlock and Livelock with Android examples (e.g., two threads accessing shared resources).
questions.title: Explain the concept of Deadlock and Livelock with Android examples (e.g., two threads accessing shared resources).

sections.heading: Deadlock & Livelock in Android
items: Deadlock: two threads wait for each other's locks → never proceed

sections.heading: Deadlock & Livelock in Android
sections.code: Thread1 locks A then B, Thread2 locks B then A → deadlock

sections.heading: Deadlock & Livelock in Android
items: Livelock: threads active but cannot make progress (constantly yielding)
Android example: two threads updating shared DB rows or resources simultaneously
Solution: proper lock ordering, use tryLock, or higher-level constructs like Executors
